heroOverview: Chloasma — also called melasma — is an acquired disorder of skin pigmentation in which melanocytes produce excess melanin, creating symmetrical, irregular brown or grey-brown patches on sun-exposed areas, predominantly the face. It affects an estimated 1-5% of the general population globally, with rates up to 50% among pregnant women in some populations, earning the colloquial name 'mask of pregnancy.' The malar cheeks, forehead, upper lip, and chin are the most common sites. Ultraviolet radiation, hormonal fluctuations — especially from oestrogen and progesterone — and genetic predisposition are the principal drivers. Although entirely benign, chloasma causes significant cosmetic distress and psychological burden. Treatment requires sustained photoprotection combined with topical depigmenting agents; relapse after sun exposure or hormonal change is common and expected.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects an estimated 1-5% of the general global population; prevalence reaches up to 50% in pregnant women in some high-UV populations (Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Women account for approximately 90% of cases; the condition is more common and more severe in people with skin phototypes III-V (Fitzpatrick scale)
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly presents in women aged 20-40 during reproductive years; can persist indefinitely after menopause
keyStats.globalCases: Worldwide prevalence estimated at over 45 million people; highest in Latin American, Southeast Asian, and Middle Eastern populations

definition: Chloasma (ICD-10: L81.1), synonymous with melasma, is a common acquired hypermelanosis characterised by symmetrical, irregular brown, dark brown, or blue-grey macules and patches on chronically sun-exposed skin, most often the face. The excess pigmentation arises from increased activity of epidermal melanocytes, which produce and transfer larger and more numerous melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes than normal. Histologically, chloasma is classified as epidermal (pigment in the epidermis, responsive to treatment), dermal (melanin-laden macrophages in the upper dermis, less responsive), or mixed (both layers, the most common). A solar lentigo or dermal accumulation pattern can also produce a fourth, indeterminate pattern. The condition is driven by three principal triggers — ultraviolet radiation, hormonal stimulation (oestrogen, progesterone, thyroid hormones), and genetic melanocyte sensitivity — which activate melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) pathways, upregulate tyrosinase enzyme activity, and increase melanin synthesis. Chloasma falls under dermatology and is classified as a disorder of hyperpigmentation. It is benign and carries no malignant potential, but its visibility on the face creates cosmetic and psychosocial consequences that make it one of the most common reasons patients seek dermatological care globally.

typesClassification.description: Melanin is deposited in the basal and suprabasal layers of the epidermis; patches appear brown under natural light and enhance markedly under Wood's lamp. This form responds best to topical depigmenting agents and chemical peels.

Melanin-laden macrophages accumulate in the papillary and reticular dermis; patches appear grey-blue or grey-brown and show minimal enhancement under Wood's lamp. Responds poorly to topical treatment and requires laser or procedural approaches.
Both epidermal and dermal pigment present simultaneously; the most common pattern clinically. Partial response to topical agents is expected, with laser treatments targeting the dermal component.
Patches on the forehead, nose, upper lip, chin, and central cheeks — the most common distribution, accounting for about 65% of cases.
Symmetrical patches confined to the cheeks and nasal bridge, without forehead or chin involvement; the second most common distribution.
Pigmentation along the ramus and body of the mandible in a bilateral distribution; less common and associated with hormonal triggers.

primarySymptoms: Symmetrical brown, dark brown, or grey-brown patches on sun-exposed facial skin, characteristically with irregular and feathered borders that do not respect anatomical lines.
Patches on the malar cheeks — often the first and most visible site — blending across the nasal bridge in a butterfly-wing pattern resembling the distribution of systemic lupus.
Forehead involvement with a band-like or irregular patch following the hairline, which typically darkens in summer and fades partially in winter.
Upper lip hyperpigmentation producing a moustache-like distribution above the vermilion border, a pattern that patients often find particularly distressing.
Chin patches that merge with neck pigmentation in moderate-to-severe cases, extending the discolouration below the jaw.
Darkening or worsening of all patches with cumulative sun exposure, even brief outdoor time without protection, due to ultraviolet activation of melanogenesis.
Visible worsening during pregnancy or while using combined oral contraceptives, reflecting the hormonal trigger of increased oestrogen and progesterone.
Asymmetric worsening on the side of the face with greater sun exposure — for example, the left cheek in left-hand-drive vehicle users.
Patches that are flat and non-palpable — chloasma does not raise the skin or produce scaling, induration, or altered texture, distinguishing it from inflammatory conditions.
Psychological symptoms including embarrassment, reduced self-confidence, and avoidance of social activities, which studies consistently identify as major components of disease burden.
earlyWarningSigns: Subtle uniform darkening of the cheeks during the first trimester of pregnancy or in the first months of oral contraceptive use.
A faint tan that persists on the forehead or malar area through winter when other sun-exposed skin has faded.
Gradual darkening of an area of skin that was previously only slightly tanned after repeated sun exposures without adequate photoprotection.
Patchy discolouration of the upper lip developing in summer, initially dismissed as a suntan.
emergencySigns: A rapidly enlarging pigmented lesion with raised borders, irregular surface, or associated bleeding — features suggesting a melanocytic lesion that requires prompt dermatological assessment to exclude melanoma rather than chloasma.

Any facial hyperpigmented lesion accompanied by systemic features such as joint pain, photosensitive rash in the butterfly distribution, or fatigue, which raises the possibility of lupus erythematosus requiring urgent evaluation.
Sudden onset of widespread facial pigmentation with mucous membrane involvement, which could indicate Addison's disease or another adrenal insufficiency requiring same-day investigation.

causes.description: UV-A, UV-B, and visible light (particularly blue light) directly stimulate melanocyte proliferation and tyrosinase activity in susceptible individuals. Even brief sun exposure can trigger or worsen episodes; visible light penetrates standard UV-only sunscreens, explaining why broad-spectrum protection with iron oxides is recommended.

causes.cause: Hormonal stimulation by oestrogen and progesterone
causes.description: Oestrogen and progesterone upregulate melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptors and directly promote melanin synthesis. Combined oral contraceptives, hormone replacement therapy, and the hormonal surge of pregnancy all trigger or worsen chloasma; progesterone-only contraceptives appear to carry a lower risk.

A family history of chloasma is present in approximately 40-50% of affected patients. Individuals with Fitzpatrick skin types III-V (brown to dark skin) have a higher baseline melanocyte activity and a stronger hypermelanotic response to UV and hormonal stimuli, explaining the higher prevalence in these populations.

Thyroid hormones directly influence melanocyte activity; hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism have both been associated with worsening melasma in some studies. A thyroid screen is often included in the workup of patients with chloasma that is resistant to standard treatment.

Certain perfumes, cosmetics, and topical products contain photosensitising agents that, when applied to the skin before sun exposure, increase local melanin production. Berloque dermatitis — a pattern of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation from psoralen-containing colognes — can produce facial pigmentation mimicking chloasma.

causes.cause: Photodynamic activation of reactive oxygen species
causes.description: Heat, infrared radiation, and oxidative stress from photodynamic processes can independently stimulate melanocytes through pathways distinct from direct UV damage, explaining why chloasma worsens in kitchen workers or others with prolonged heat exposure even under covered skin.

riskFactors.description: Up to 50% of pregnant women in high-UV populations develop chloasma driven by rising oestrogen and progesterone; it may fade postpartum but persists in about one third of affected women.
Exogenous oestrogen and progesterone in combined pills trigger chloasma in genetically susceptible users; switching to a progesterone-only preparation or non-hormonal contraception can reduce risk.

Darker-skinned individuals have more melanogenically active melanocytes that respond more briskly to UV and hormonal signals; prevalence in these phototypes is several times higher than in phototype I-II individuals.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic or high cumulative UV exposure
riskFactors.description: Sun-seeking behaviour, outdoor occupation, and living at lower latitudes where UV intensity is higher all increase lifetime UV dose, the primary environmental driver of chloasma onset and relapse.

riskFactors.factor: Positive family history of chloasma or melasma
riskFactors.description: A first-degree family history is reported in 40-50% of patients and is the single strongest predictor of personal risk, pointing to heritable melanocyte sensitivity.
Autoimmune thyroid disorders are more prevalent in women with chloasma than in the general population; treatment of underlying thyroid dysfunction may improve pigmentation in some patients.

riskFactors.factor: Use of phototoxic cosmetics or topical products
riskFactors.description: Fragrances, essential oils, and certain cosmetics containing furocoumarins or other photosensitisers increase melanin synthesis when the skin is exposed to sunlight.

Computer screens, LED lighting, and cooking heat are sources of visible and infrared light that can stimulate melanocytes in susceptible individuals; occupational exposure compounds the risk from solar UV.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 20-40 during reproductive years, representing approximately 90% of all cases
Pregnant women, particularly those with Fitzpatrick skin types III-V, with prevalence up to 50% in some populations
Users of combined oral contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy
Individuals with brown to dark skin tones (Fitzpatrick types III-V) regardless of geographic location
Latin American, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, and South Asian populations where phototype III-V predominates and UV intensity is high
Women with a first-degree relative affected by chloasma or melasma
diagnosisOverview: Chloasma is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic appearance and distribution of facial hyperpigmented patches, the patient's sex, age, hormonal status, and sun-exposure history. A dermatologist examines the face in natural light and with a Wood's lamp (ultraviolet A illumination), which enhances epidermal melanin (patches appear darker) but does not enhance dermal pigment, providing an indication of the histological depth and likely treatment responsiveness. Reflectance confocal microscopy, when available, can non-invasively characterise pigment depth and melanocyte distribution without biopsy. A skin biopsy is rarely required but may be performed when the clinical diagnosis is uncertain, particularly to exclude other pigmentary disorders, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, or drug-induced pigmentation. The Melasma Area and Severity Index (MASI) and the modified MASI are validated scoring tools used in clinical practice and trials to quantify the area, darkness, and homogeneity of patches before and after treatment. The principal diagnoses to exclude are post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (history of preceding inflammation at the site), drug-induced pigmentation (amiodarone, minocycline, antimalarials), solar lentigines (discrete, smaller spots, not coalescing patches), and, importantly, discoid lupus erythematosus, which can produce facial hyperpigmentation in a butterfly distribution with associated scaling and follicular plugging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates epidermal from dermal chloasma; epidermal pigment enhances under Wood's lamp, guiding treatment selection and predicting responsiveness to topical agents.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld UV lamp is held 10-15 cm from the face in a darkened room; the examination takes 2-3 minutes and is entirely painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Melasma Area and Severity Index (MASI) scoring
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the area and darkness of patches to establish a baseline and objectively monitor treatment response at follow-up visits.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist visually scores four facial areas; the scoring takes 5-10 minutes and requires no equipment or patient preparation.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy with histopathology and Fontana-Masson staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms epidermal or dermal melanin distribution when the clinical and Wood's lamp assessment is ambiguous or when an alternative diagnosis must be excluded.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3-4 mm punch biopsy taken under local anaesthetic; one or two sutures are placed and histology results are available in 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the pattern of pigmentation (reticular, pseudoreticular) and assesses telangiectasias that indicate the dermal component, aiding subtype classification without biopsy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld dermoscope with polarised light is pressed gently against the skin; the examination is immediate, non-invasive, and takes 2-5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T3, free T4)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for thyroid dysfunction, which is more common in women with chloasma and may be an independent driver of melanocyte stimulation in refractory cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluated in perimenopausal women or those with irregular cycles to determine whether endocrine changes are contributing to persistent or worsening pigmentation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Timed blood draw on specific days of the menstrual cycle if relevant; results typically within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Chloasma treatment requires a multimodal, sustained approach because the underlying melanocytic sensitivity is not corrected — only suppressed — and relapse with sun exposure is predictable. Daily broad-spectrum photoprotection with SPF 30-50+ sunscreen containing iron oxides (to block visible light) is the single most important intervention and must be continued indefinitely regardless of other treatments. Without photoprotection, any depigmenting agent will be outpaced by ongoing UV stimulation. The first-line topical treatment is triple combination therapy: hydroquinone 4% combined with a topical retinoid (tretinoin 0.05%) and a mild topical corticosteroid (fluocinolone acetonide 0.01%), as in the licensed Kligman-Willis formula. This combination addresses all three major steps of melanogenesis simultaneously and achieves measurable improvement in 60-80% of patients at 8 weeks in controlled trials. Hydroquinone monotherapy (2-4%) is an alternative and is available over the counter at lower concentrations. Second-line topical agents include azelaic acid 15-20%, tranexamic acid (topical 5% or oral 250 mg twice daily), kojic acid, niacinamide 4-5%, and alpha-arbutin, which are particularly useful when hydroquinone is not tolerated or in patients wishing to avoid prolonged hydroquinone use. Chemical peels with glycolic acid, salicylic acid, or trichloroacetic acid can accelerate epidermal pigment clearance as an adjunct to topical therapy. Laser treatments — Q-switched Nd:YAG, picosecond lasers, and low-fluence intense pulsed light — target the dermal component and mixed-type chloasma, though the risk of paradoxical post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) from laser use requires specialist experience and cautious settings, particularly in darker skin types. Hormonal adjustment — switching from combined to progesterone-only or non-hormonal contraception — can reduce the hormonal stimulus and improve long-term outcomes. Oral tranexamic acid, 250 mg twice daily, is gaining evidence as a systemic adjunct for refractory melasma, with trials showing significant improvement over placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Triple combination cream (hydroquinone 4% + tretinoin 0.05% + fluocinolone acetonide 0.01%)
medicalTreatments.description: The licensed Kligman-Willis formula addresses tyrosinase inhibition, melanin precursor oxidation, and keratinocyte turnover simultaneously. Applied once daily to affected areas for 8 weeks, then as maintenance at reduced frequency. Long-term hydroquinone use beyond 6 months is generally avoided to minimise risk of ochronosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% of patients achieve clinically meaningful improvement in MASI scores at 8 weeks; superiority over dual and monotherapy demonstrated in multiple randomised controlled trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Azelaic acid (15-20% cream or gel)
medicalTreatments.description: A naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid that inhibits tyrosinase and is cytotoxic to hyperactive melanocytes while sparing normal melanocytes. Safe in pregnancy at 20% concentration under medical supervision; effective as maintenance after hydroquinone induction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies show 65-70% of patients achieve good to excellent improvement with twice-daily 20% azelaic acid over 24 weeks; comparable to hydroquinone 4% in several trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical tranexamic acid (5% solution or cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits plasminogen activator in keratinocytes, reducing the ultraviolet-induced activation of melanocytes via the arachidonic acid pathway. Applied twice daily; good tolerability and suitable for maintenance and for patients who prefer to avoid hydroquinone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials demonstrate approximately 44% reduction in MASI score versus baseline with 12 weeks of topical tranexamic acid 5%.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral tranexamic acid (250 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic inhibition of plasminogen activator reduces melanocyte stimulation through a cutaneous and endocrine mechanism. Used for refractory chloasma; generally prescribed for 2-6 months with monitoring for thromboembolic risk. Not used in patients with thrombophilia or a history of venous thromboembolism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Multiple randomised trials, including a 2019 Cochrane-level meta-analysis, show approximately 30-50% MASI reduction at 12 weeks compared with placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Retinoids (topical tretinoin 0.025-0.1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Tretinoin accelerates epidermal turnover, disperses melanin granules, and inhibits tyrosinase transcription. Used as part of triple therapy or as monotherapy; contraindicated in pregnancy. Initial irritation (retinoid dermatitis) is common and managed by starting at lower concentrations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tretinoin monotherapy achieves approximately 50% improvement in mild to moderate epidermal chloasma at 40 weeks based on randomised trials; greatest value as part of triple combination therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Chemical peels (glycolic acid 20-70%, salicylic acid 20-30%)
medicalTreatments.description: Superficial to medium-depth peels accelerate desquamation of melanin-laden keratinocytes and enhance penetration of topical agents. Performed in a series of 4-6 treatments at 2-4 week intervals, always as an adjunct to strict photoprotection and topical depigmentation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Series of glycolic acid peels as an adjunct to topical agents achieve 30-50% additional reduction in MASI score versus topical treatment alone in comparative trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Niacinamide 4-5% topical
alternativeTreatments.description: Inhibits melanosome transfer from melanocytes to keratinocytes without inhibiting melanin synthesis itself; gentle enough for daily use and as adjunct to other agents, with a favourable tolerability profile in sensitive skin.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid 10-20% serum)
alternativeTreatments.description: Acts as a reducing agent that inhibits tyrosinase and scavenges reactive oxygen species generated by UV exposure. Stabilised formulations have the best evidence; combined with retinoids and photoprotection for a synergistic depigmenting effect.

alternativeTreatments.name: Laser and light-based therapy (low-fluence Q-switched Nd:YAG)
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets dermal melanin and melanosomes with minimal epidermal damage. Performed in series of 6-10 sessions weekly or bi-weekly; requires specialist experience in darker skin types to avoid paradoxical PIH. Best used after adequate topical treatment for the epidermal component.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should evaluate chloasma when over-the-counter products have not produced improvement after 8-12 weeks, when the diagnosis is uncertain and a biopsy may be needed, or when procedural options such as chemical peels, Q-switched laser, or prescription triple combination therapy are being considered. A specialist can also exclude pigmentary disorders that require different management and counsel on the long-term photopro tection strategy essential for sustained remission.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a dermatologist with specific experience in pigmentary disorders and darker skin phototypes (III-V), as treatment decisions — particularly around laser thresholds and peel depths — differ significantly based on the risk of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in darker skin. A practice offering dermoscopy, Wood's lamp assessment, and a range of procedural options including chemical peels and laser is ideal. Confirm that the clinician is familiar with the evidence on oral tranexamic acid and progesterone-only contraceptive switching for hormonally driven cases.
preventionStrategies: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen with iron oxides (tinted sunscreen) daily, including on cloudy days and in winter, to block both UV and visible light; reapply every 2 hours of outdoor exposure.
Wear wide-brimmed hats and UV-protective clothing to reduce the total facial UV dose, particularly during peak sun hours (10 am to 4 pm).
Switch from combined oral contraceptives to progesterone-only or non-hormonal methods if chloasma developed on combined pills, to remove the oestrogenic hormonal stimulus.
Begin topical depigmenting therapy before planned pregnancy if you have a prior history of pregnancy-induced chloasma, under medical guidance.
Avoid phototoxic topical products including psoralen-containing fragrances and certain retinol formulations that increase UV sensitivity.
Seek shade and limit blue light exposure from screens and LED lights by using anti-blue-light screen protectors or switching screen settings, particularly in the evening.
lifestyleModifications: Make SPF application a daily morning step regardless of planned sun exposure; consistent use is more effective than intense protection only on sunny days.
Use tinted sunscreens or mineral make-up with iron oxides to add visible light protection beyond standard UV filters.
Reapply sunscreen every 2 hours and after sweating, swimming, or facial washing when outdoors.
Avoid waxing facial hair at affected pigmented areas; waxing can trigger post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation on sensitised skin.
Wash and rinse the face with lukewarm rather than hot water; heat independently stimulates melanocyte activity.
Review and discontinue phototoxic cosmetics, particularly perfumes applied to sun-exposed skin before outdoor activities.
Support general skin health with antioxidant-rich foods and adequate sleep, which can reduce background oxidative stress driving melanogenesis.

recommended: Antioxidant-rich foods: tomatoes, leafy greens, red and yellow peppers, and berries, which combat the reactive oxygen species that drive melanogenesis
Vitamin C from dietary sources (citrus, kiwi, broccoli) to support cutaneous antioxidant defence
Polyphenol-rich foods such as green tea, which have demonstrated modest anti-melanogenic effects in in vitro studies
Adequate water intake to maintain skin hydration and barrier function
avoid: Large quantities of photosensitising foods such as certain celery, parsnip, and fig extracts if applied to skin before sun exposure (relevant mainly to topical contact, not ingestion)
Excess alcohol, which can worsen facial erythema and increase UV sensitivity
Very high-dose beta-carotene supplements, which can produce a yellow-orange discolouration that may confound assessment of pigmentation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged and does not worsen chloasma provided adequate photoprotection is applied before outdoor activity. Exercise-induced sweating can reduce sunscreen efficacy; use a water-resistant SPF 50+ product and reapply after the session. Outdoor exercise during peak UV hours (10 am to 4 pm) should be minimised; early morning or evening activity reduces UV dose substantially. Indoor exercise carries no specific limitation.
dailyManagement: Apply tinted broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen as the final morning skincare step, every day.
Use prescribed topical agents (hydroquinone, azelaic acid, retinoid) consistently in the evening; irregular application significantly reduces efficacy.
Track pigmentation changes monthly with standard lighting photos to assess treatment progress objectively.
Attend scheduled dermatology follow-ups to adjust the depigmenting regimen seasonally — typically lighter maintenance in winter, more intensive treatment in summer.
Report any new lesions with raised borders, bleeding, or changed sensation promptly to exclude a coincidental melanocytic lesion requiring biopsy.
prognosis: Chloasma is a chronic relapsing condition without a permanent cure, but it responds well to sustained treatment combined with strict photoprotection. The majority of patients with epidermal chloasma achieve satisfactory lightening with first-line triple combination therapy; dermal and mixed types are more resistant and may require procedural interventions. Pregnancy-related chloasma often fades partially in the postpartum period but persists in approximately one third of women at 12 months. Contraceptive-induced chloasma typically improves after hormonal method discontinuation but full clearance is not guaranteed. Relapse after sun exposure is predictable and should be anticipated rather than regarded as treatment failure; maintenance topical therapy and daily photoprotection are required indefinitely in most patients. The condition carries no risk of malignant transformation and does not affect physical health, but the psychological burden — including reduced self-confidence, social withdrawal, and depression — is substantial in a significant minority of patients and warrants attention as part of comprehensive care.
recoveryTimeline: With consistent use of triple combination cream plus strict photoprotection, most patients see meaningful lightening of epidermal patches within 6-8 weeks. Moderate to significant improvement of MASI score typically occurs at 3-4 months. Dermal or mixed chloasma requires longer treatment, often 6-12 months with combination topical and procedural approaches. Complete clearance is uncommon; treatment aims for maximal lightening and stable maintenance rather than cure. Relapse after treatment discontinuation or increased sun exposure can occur within weeks, making photoprotection a lifelong commitment.
complications: Relapse after sun exposure or hormonal change, which occurs in the majority of patients who discontinue photoprotection, underscoring the need for indefinite maintenance.

Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation worsened by aggressive chemical peels or laser treatments performed without adequate pre-treatment and post-procedure sun protection, particularly in skin types IV-VI.

Exogenous ochronosis — paradoxical bluish-black discolouration of the dermis — from very prolonged, high-concentration hydroquinone use (years of high-dose application), primarily reported in darker skin types using unregulated products.

Psychological complications including depression, social anxiety, and reduced quality of life scores comparable to those seen in chronic skin diseases such as psoriasis, based on Dermatology Life Quality Index data.
Contact sensitisation to topical agents, particularly fragrances and preservatives in cosmetic formulations used to cover or treat the pigmentation.
Masking of a coincidental melanocytic lesion by the patient or clinician attributing any new facial pigmentation to chloasma, delaying melanoma diagnosis in rare cases.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Melasma
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of melasma (chloasma) from the leading US dermatology professional body, covering photoprotection, treatments, and when to see a dermatologist.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Melasma patient information leaflet
supportResources.description: UK-based patient information on melasma diagnosis, treatment, and prevention, produced and reviewed by consultant dermatologists.

supportResources.name: NHS — Melasma
supportResources.description: NHS patient guidance on melasma symptoms, treatment options available on the NHS, and self-care measures.

sources.title: Sheth VM, Pandya AG. Melasma: a comprehensive update. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2011
Passeron T, Picardo M. Melasma, a photoaging disorder. Pigment Cell & Melanoma Research, 2018
Rajanala S et al. Freckles and melasma: a comparison of dermatoscopic features. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2019
McKesey J, Tovar-Garza A, Pandya AG. Melasma treatment: an evidence-based review. American Journal of Clinical Dermatology, 2020
Taraz M et al. Tranexamic acid in melasma: a review. Clinical, Cosmetic and Investigational Dermatology, 2017
AAD — Clinical practice guideline for melasma

confusedWithConditions.name: Freckles
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Freckles are small, discrete, uniformly brown macules that darken with sun exposure and fade in winter; chloasma produces larger, coalescing, irregular patches that are driven by both UV and hormonal factors.

confusedWithConditions.name: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation follows a specific preceding skin injury or inflammation at the affected site; chloasma appears on intact skin and is driven by UV and hormonal stimulation rather than prior inflammation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Actinic keratosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Actinic keratosis is a rough, scaly precancerous lesion from UV damage, not a smooth pigmented patch; the texture and malignant potential of actinic keratosis differ fundamentally from the flat pigmentation of chloasma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Discoid lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Discoid lupus produces facial patches that may hyperpigment but also show scaling, follicular plugging, scarring, and erythema absent in chloasma; biopsy and systemic assessment differentiate the two.

relatedConditions.name: Freckles
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are melanocyte-driven facial pigmentation disorders; freckles are genetically determined and UV-enhanced while chloasma additionally requires hormonal triggers for full expression.

relatedConditions.name: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
relatedConditions.relevance: Both present as facial hyperpigmentation and are treated with overlapping agents (hydroquinone, azelaic acid); distinguishing the two guides whether treating an underlying inflammatory condition is also required.

relatedConditions.name: Vitiligo
relatedConditions.relevance: Vitiligo and chloasma represent opposite ends of the melanocyte activity spectrum and can coexist; both are assessed and managed by dermatologists with expertise in pigmentary disorders.

relatedConditions.name: Actinic keratosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Actinic keratosis and chloasma both result from cumulative UV damage to facial skin; patients with chloasma may also develop actinic keratoses and require monitoring for these premalignant lesions.

faqs.question: What is chloasma?
faqs.answer: Chloasma, also called melasma, is a common skin condition that produces symmetrical brown or grey-brown patches on sun-exposed areas of the face. It is caused by overactive melanocytes producing too much pigment, triggered by UV light and hormonal changes. It is benign but can cause significant cosmetic distress.

faqs.question: Is chloasma the same as melasma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chloasma and melasma are two names for the same condition. Chloasma is the older term and is often used specifically for pregnancy-related facial hyperpigmentation (hence 'mask of pregnancy'), while melasma is the broader current medical term used regardless of cause.

faqs.question: What causes chloasma?
faqs.answer: Chloasma is triggered by three main factors: UV and visible light exposure, hormonal stimulation from oestrogen and progesterone, and genetic predisposition. It commonly appears during pregnancy, with oral contraceptive use, or after prolonged sun exposure in susceptible individuals with darker skin tones.

faqs.question: Does chloasma go away after pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy-related chloasma often fades partially after delivery as hormone levels normalise, but it persists in approximately one third of women at 12 months postpartum. Continued UV exposure can prevent full fading. Consistent daily sunscreen use and, if needed, topical depigmenting agents speed recovery after delivery.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for chloasma?
faqs.answer: Daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen with iron oxides is the most important step. First-line topical treatment is triple combination cream containing hydroquinone 4%, tretinoin 0.05%, and a mild steroid. Azelaic acid, tranexamic acid, and chemical peels are alternatives. All treatments require strict ongoing photoprotection to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: How long does it take for chloasma to fade?
faqs.answer: With consistent use of triple combination cream and daily sunscreen, most patients see meaningful lightening of epidermal patches within 6-8 weeks and significant improvement at 3-4 months. Dermal or mixed types take longer — often 6-12 months. Complete clearance is uncommon; maintenance treatment and lifelong photoprotection are usually needed.

faqs.question: Can sunscreen alone treat chloasma?
faqs.answer: Sunscreen alone cannot lighten existing patches, but it is essential to prevent worsening and to allow other treatments to work. Without daily broad-spectrum photoprotection, any depigmenting cream will be overcome by continued UV-driven melanin production. Use of SPF 50+ with iron oxides every morning is non-negotiable alongside any active treatment.

faqs.question: Will stopping the contraceptive pill improve chloasma?
faqs.answer: Switching from a combined pill (oestrogen and progesterone) to a progesterone-only or non-hormonal contraceptive removes the oestrogenic trigger and often leads to gradual improvement over months. However, improvement is not guaranteed and sun-induced patches may persist independently of the hormonal component.

faqs.question: Is chloasma dangerous or does it become skin cancer?
faqs.answer: Chloasma carries no malignant potential and does not develop into skin cancer. It is entirely benign. However, any facial pigmented lesion with raised borders, irregular surface, bleeding, or rapid change should be assessed by a dermatologist to exclude a melanocytic lesion, since chloasma and melanoma can occasionally be confused on visual inspection alone.

faqs.question: Does hydroquinone cream work for chloasma?
faqs.answer: Hydroquinone 4% is a first-line depigmenting agent that inhibits tyrosinase and melanin production. It achieves good improvement in most patients with epidermal chloasma within 8-12 weeks, particularly in the triple combination formula. Prolonged use beyond 4-6 months is generally avoided to minimise the small risk of ochronosis.

faqs.question: Can men get chloasma?
faqs.answer: Yes, though men represent only about 10% of cases. Male chloasma typically follows prolonged UV exposure rather than hormonal triggers, and the distribution and treatment approach are the same as in women. A hormonal cause is evaluated if chloasma appears in a young man without a history of significant sun exposure.

faqs.question: Does chloasma affect the body beyond the face?
faqs.answer: Chloasma predominantly affects the face but can extend to the neck and forearms in some patients, particularly those with a history of high UV exposure at these sites. The condition follows the distribution of sun-exposed skin and is rarely seen on unexposed areas.

faqs.question: Is laser treatment safe for chloasma?
faqs.answer: Laser treatment can be effective for dermal and mixed chloasma that has not responded to topical agents, but it carries a risk of paradoxical post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, particularly in darker skin types. Low-fluence Q-switched Nd:YAG and picosecond lasers have the most evidence and lowest risk when performed by experienced dermatologists.

faqs.question: What toner or serum is best for chloasma?
faqs.answer: Evidence-based topical ingredients include niacinamide 4-5%, vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid 10-20%), alpha-arbutin, and tranexamic acid. These inhibit different steps in the melanin production and transfer pathway and can be layered with prescription depigmenting agents. Use antioxidant serums in the morning under sunscreen for maximum benefit.

faqs.question: Does oral tranexamic acid work for chloasma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral tranexamic acid 250 mg twice daily has been shown in multiple randomised trials to reduce MASI scores by 30-50% over 12 weeks. It works by inhibiting plasminogen activator, reducing UV-triggered melanocyte stimulation. It is prescribed for refractory cases and monitored for thromboembolic risk.

faqs.question: How is chloasma different from freckles?
faqs.answer: Freckles are small, round, discrete brown macules that are genetically determined and fade in winter; they do not require hormonal triggers. Chloasma produces larger, irregular, coalescing patches driven by both UV and hormonal factors and does not fade as completely in winter without active treatment.

faqs.question: Can make-up or cosmetics cause chloasma?
faqs.answer: Certain fragrances and cosmetics containing photosensitising agents can trigger or worsen facial pigmentation with sun exposure. Berloque dermatitis from psoralen-containing perfumes is a related pattern. Switching to fragrance-free, non-comedogenic products is advisable for patients prone to chloasma.

faqs.question: Why does chloasma come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Chloasma recurs because the underlying melanocyte sensitivity is not cured — only suppressed. Any resumption of UV exposure, hormonal change, or phototoxic product use can reactivate melanogenesis. Maintenance topical treatment in summer and daily SPF 50+ sunscreen year-round are required to prevent relapse after successful clearance.

faqs.question: Is chloasma worse in summer?
faqs.answer: Yes. UV and visible light exposure in summer significantly intensifies melanin production in susceptible melanocytes, causing visible darkening of patches. Strict photoprotection during summer — high-SPF tinted sunscreen, hats, shade — and seasonal adjustment of topical treatment intensity are key management principles.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for chloasma?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist if over-the-counter products have not produced visible improvement after 8-12 weeks, if the diagnosis is uncertain, if a lesion has changed shape or texture, or if you want prescription-strength treatment or a procedural option such as a chemical peel or laser. A specialist can also identify any co-existing pigmentary condition.

metaDescription: Chloasma causes brown facial patches from UV and hormones. Learn causes, diagnosis, and treatments including sunscreen and depigmenting creams. Doctor-reviewed.
